coredata.dc:title: Room temperature pH-dependent ammonia gas sensors using graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wei
Li, Fushan
Ooi, Poh Choon
Ye, Yun
Kim, Tae Whan
Guo, Tailiang
coredata.dc:description: We report a simple solution-process route to realize ammonia (NH3) gas sensor based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Transmission electron microscopy analysis confirmed that the 8–10nm GQDs were formed from multi-walled carbon nanotubes by using ultrasonication treatment. The as-fabricated gas sensor showed promising selectivity response when expose to NH3 ambient at room temperature. It is indicated that by adjusting the pH value of the aqueous GQDs in acidic and neutral, two types of gas sensors with contrary current responses could be obtained, which might be resulted from quantum confinement, edge effects and presence of functional groups on GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
MWCNTs
Ammonia gas sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province ( 2013J01233 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61377027 ), National Research Foundation of Korea ( 2013-016467 ), State Key Laboratory of Electronic Thin Films and Integrated Devices (Grant KFJJ201309 ) and Foundation of Fuzhou University ( 2011-XY-24 ). Wei Chen received his Master of Engineering degree in Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics in 2007 from Fuzhou University. He is presently a Ph.D. student in Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics at Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China. His research interest comprises the development of new nanomaterials for application in electrochemical sensors and optoelectronic devices. Fushan Li is presently a full professor in College of Physics and Information Engineering at Fuzhou University. He received his Ph.D. in Department of Physics at Peking University in 2005. His research interest focuses on the development of novel nanomaterials and their applications in optoelectronic devices. Poh Choon Ooi received his Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering in 2013 from the Auckland University, New Zealand. He is presently a Post doctorate at Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China. His research interest comprises the theoretical and applied research in the field of polymer electronics, fabrications of nanoparticles and nanostructures. Yun Ye is presently a professor at Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China. She received her Ph.D. at the University of Electronic Science and Technology of China in 2007. Her research comprises nano-electronic materials and devices. Tae Whan Kim is a professor in Department of Electronic Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, Korea. He received his Ph.D. in Department of Physics from State University of New York at Buffalo in 1989. His research comprises nano-electronic materials and devices. Tailiang Guo is a professor of Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China. He received his Master of Engineering degree at Fuzhou University in 1989. His research comprises nano-electronic materials and devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of original MWCNTs; (b) TEM image showing the broken MWCNTs after acidic oxidation. (c) TEM image of pancake carbon nanosheet derived from broken MWCNTs. Inset: A single GQD derived from the pancake nanosheet. (d) TEM image of GQDs after centrifugation; (e) enlarged TEM image of the GQDs; (f) PL spectra of the aqueous GQDs with pH=5 at various excitation wavelengths; (g) PL spectra of the aqueous GQDs with pH=7 at various excitation wavelengths; (h) FTIR of the original MWCNTs, samples A and B.
(a) Schematic structure of the gas sensors. (b) AFM surface topography of the gas sensor and (c) 3D AFM topography of the gas sensor.

(a) 10ppm ammonia response characteristic of sensor A. (b) 10ppm ammonia response characteristic of sensor B. (c) Response of sensor A to 10ppm NH3 for three cycles. (d) Response of sensor B to 10ppm NH3 for three cycles. (e) Current response behavior for sensor A at different concentrations of NH3 ambient. (f) Current response behavior for sensor B at different concentrations of NH3 ambient. (g) Absolute response of sensors A and B at various concentrations of NH3. (h) Absolute response of sensors A and B to 10ppm NH3 at various relative humidity.
Schematics showing the sensing mechanism of the sensors to NH3. (a) Sensor A. (b) Sensor B.
Summary of gas response of sensors A and B when exposed to 500ppm CO, H2 and C4H10 ambient.

head.ce:title: Room temperature pH-dependent ammonia gas sensors using graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Optoelectronic Technology, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350002, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Optoelectronic Technology, Fuzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic Engineering, Hanyang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a simple solution-process route to realize ammonia (NH3) gas sensor based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Transmission electron microscopy analysis confirmed that the 8–10nm GQDs were formed from multi-walled carbon nanotubes by using ultrasonication treatment. The as-fabricated gas sensor showed promising selectivity response when expose to NH3 ambient at room temperature. It is indicated that by adjusting the pH value of the aqueous GQDs in acidic and neutral, two types of gas sensors with contrary current responses could be obtained, which might be resulted from quantum confinement, edge effects and presence of functional groups on GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the 0D nanomaterial with single or few-layer graphene, has emerged as a high potential material owing to its fascinating physical properties [1]. The properties of GQDs such as energy band gap, photoluminescence and electron transport ability will vary when their size is changed. Due to these unique properties, GQDs have attracted extensive attention from scientists in the fields of chemistry, physics, materials, biology, and other interdisciplinary sciences to explore the possible applications. For example, in energy related applications, GQDs have been used as active layer or counter electrode to improve the performance of solar cells [2]. Sensor devices have received broad attention due to their application in various industry fields [3]. And there are also some studies of GQDs being used in sensor devices, such as electronic sensor [4], photoluminescence sensor [5], electrochemiluminescence sensors [6], and electrochemical sensor [7]. Even though GQDs are being widely used in the field of sensors, however, it is rarely reported for applications of GQDs in ammonia gas sensor, which is one of most common gas sensors and has been widely used in food processing, breath analysis for medical diagnoses, and environmental monitoring [8,9].

In comparison with the ammonia gas sensor made of metal oxides, such as SnO2, WO3 and ZnO, which need elevated operating temperatures in the range from 200 to 400°C in order to activate the adsorption and desorption of ammonia [10,11], gas sensors based on carbon nanomaterials can operate at room temperature [12,13]. As reported previously [13,14], graphene oxide shows sensitivity to detect ammonia, nevertheless the response or recovery times to ammonia are still under performance at room temperature. Hence, in this study, a simple solution-based spray coating method is demonstrated to fabricate ammonia gas sensor based on GQDs with functional groups, which is incorporated to improve the response of the device. It is found that the large surface-to-volume ratio of GQDs tends to improve the response and recovery speed to ammonia and GQDs prepared under different pH values lead to different resistance behavior when sensor was exposed to ammonia ambient. It is believed that GQDs with functional groups could play a crucial role to achieve excellent gas sensing performance.

The “top-down” approach was used to prepare GQDs in this study. Multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs, 0.06g, length 5–15μm, diameter 10–20nm, purchased from Shenzhen Nanotech Port of China) were ball milled for 6h, then were subjected to ultrasonication for 48h at 60°C in a concentrated mixture of HNO3 and H2SO4 with volume ratio 1:3. The acidic mixture was then diluted with deionized (DI) water. The diluted mixture was left standing still for 48h to obtain the phase separation of the solution, followed by top part solution extraction and filtration using a 200nm Teflon filter to eliminate the precipitation. Subsequently, the filtration solution was separated into two samples, labeled as A and B and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) for further dilution to adjust the pH level. Here, sample A was dialyzed for 2 days to achieve pH 5 whereas sample B was dialyzed for 7 days to achieve pH 7. Thereafter, both sample A and B were centrifuged at 6000rpm for 1h. The top part of both samples were extracted and concentrated at 60°C to obtain GQDs with pH 5 and pH 7.

The as-prepared GQDs showed uniform dispersion in water solution, which is beneficial to ease device fabrication. Prior to device fabrication, planar ITO electrode pairs with 200μm interval were prepared by using photo lithography technique. Then the ITO electrode pairs were cleaned by ultrasonication in acetone, ethanol, and DI water, respectively. Then, nitrogen stream was used to dry the substrate. Finally, sensor A and B were fabricated by spray coating a layer of GQDs using the as-prepared sample A and B, respectively, with the aid of nitrogen stream at 0.2MPa on top of the cleaned ITO substrate at 120°C. In this work, semiconductor testing system (Keithley, 4200-scs) was used to measure the gas sensor response characteristics. The bias voltage was set at 5V and applied to the two ITO electrodes of sensor. The response of the gas sensors based on GQDs prepared under different pH values were measured at room temperature of 25°C with different concentrations of NH3 in synthetic air with relative humidity (RH) of 50–55%. The surface topography of the as-fabricated sensors was analyzed using atomic force microscope (Bruker, Multimode 8). The morphological images of MWCNTs and GQD were taken by high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2010).

Fig. 1 shows the TEM images and photoluminescence (PL) spectra for the GQDs derived from MWCNTs followed by the Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of the sample A, B and MWCNTs. Fig. 1(a) shows the original MWCNTs before acidic oxidation. As above mentioned, MWCNTs were subjected to ultrasonication with acidic solution at 60°C, leading to the formation of small broken MWCNTs, as shown in Fig. 1(b). With longer ultrasonication process, the small broken MWCNTs could be cut into pancake carbon nanosheets, which were shown in Fig. 1(c). Further ultrasonication treatment would result in the formation of graphene quantum dots with smaller size. The as-prepared GQDs were further diluted and the distribution of GQDs after centrifugation is shown in Fig. 1(d). The diameter of the GQDs was estimated to be 8–10nm. Fig. 1(f) and (g) showed the PL spectra of the aqueous GQDs with pH 5 and pH 7 at various excitation wavelengths. The GQDs exhibited excitation dependent PL behavior. For the aqueous GQDs solution with pH 5, the PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths show blue shift in comparison with the aqueous GQDs solution with pH 7. This is in agreement with our previous report [15]. There is presence of chemical functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl and carboxyl during acidic oxidation [16]. A small number of epoxy group (COC) and a large number of carbonyl (CO) were bring to the basal planes of MWCNTs, and experienced unzipping process to break the underlying CC bonds and thereby give rise to crack [17]. The presence of chemical functional groups on GQDs for samples A and B could be identified using FTIR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1(h). The FTIR characteristic for MWCNTs without functionalization was also recorded in Fig. 1(h) as a reference spectrum for comparison. As previously reported, the GQDs and MWCNTs exhibit the CO stretching peak at 1719cm−1 [18,19]. Besides, the FTIR spectra of the oxidized samples A and B exhibit stretching peaks at 3421cm−1, 1405cm−1, and 1111cm−1, which are corresponding to OH, COO− and CO groups, respectively [20]. These results indicate the presence of OH and COOH groups attached to GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1(h), sample A exhibits a strong stretching peak at 1405cm−1, indicating abundant oxygen group of COOH on its edge after oxidation. It should be noted that during the synthesis of GQDs, carboxyl functional groups were attached at the edge of GQDs, leading to high solubility of GQDs in aqueous solution and improving the response to adsorb and desorb gas molecules on its surface. Furthermore, GQDs synthesized with oxygen chemical method can form zigzag and armchair basic geometries at GQD edges. It is proposed that the zigzag GQDs is metallic and armchair GQDs is semiconducting. With both geometries, GQDs present as semiconductor materials [21].

Fig. 2 (a) shows the schematic structure of the as-fabricated gas sensor. Fig. 2(b) and (c) shows the atomic force microscopy (AFM) surface topography of the sensor and its 3-dimensional AFM topography, respectively. It can be observed that after the long time spray coating process, the GQDs were deposited on the surface of substrate and reunited to form continuous thin film with a number of islands which bring large specific surface areas.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) shows the current response behavior for sensors A and B, respectively, when sensors were exposed and removed from ammonia ambient. It is indicated that as both sensors were exposed to NH3 gas, the electrical response behavior is obviously different. The resistance of sensor A decreased when expose to NH3 gas. In contrast, the resistance of sensor B increased when exposed to NH3 gas. Both sensors are sensitive to ammonia, and the sensor response toward NH3 gas can be determined by Eq. (1):
(1)
Response =
Δ
R /
R 0
= (
R
NH 3
−
R 0
) /
R 0
= ( 5 /
I
NH 3
− 5 /
I 0
) / ( 5 /
I 0
)
where R 0 and
R
NH 3
are the resistances before and after the exposure of the sensors to NH3, respectively. I 0 and
I
NH 3
are the current before and after the exposure of the sensors to NH3, respectively. From Eq. (1), the response of gas sensor A was estimated to be −14.9%, 90% response time is 26s and 90% recovery time is 21s. Whereas the response of gas sensor B was estimated to be 5.9%, 90% response time is 27s and 90% recovery time is 72s. Fig. 3(c) and (d) shows the responses of the sensors A and B to NH3 for three cycles. It is found that the resistance can recover its initial state after immersing into air from ammonia, indicating the high operating stability of the GQDs-based gas sensor. Fig. 3(g) shows the absolute response of sensors A and B, respectively, varying with different concentrations of NH3 ambient. The absolute response value of sensor A increases exponentially when the NH3 concentration is increased and tends to saturate when NH3 concentration is higher than 300ppm with the response at 400ppm ammonia recorded as 44.5%. However, the response of sensor B varies linearly with NH3 concentration and the response at 400ppm ammonia is about 35.1%. Hence, it is predicted that sensor B with neutral pH is able to demonstrate higher response range beyond 400ppm NH3 concentration as compared to sensor A with acidic pH 5. Nevertheless, as NH3 concentration is below 400ppm, the response of sensor A is higher than sensor B. We also test the sensor under relative humidity from 14% to 80%. As shown in Fig. 3(h), the sensor made with aqueous GQDs with pH 7 has little change of response to 10ppm ammonia under relative humidity from 14% to 80%, while the response of the sensor made with aqueous GQDs with pH 5 would change to a certain content at low relative humidity. But the sensor has little change of response to 10ppm ammonia under relative humidity from 35% to 80%.

To further confirm the gas selectivity of the as-fabricated sensors toward NH3 ambient, the selectivity detection tests were further carried out in 500ppm CO, H2 and C4H10 ambient, respectively. Table 1 summarized the gas selectivity results of both sensors. It is indicated that sensors A and B show no or low response to CO, H2 and C4H10.

Fig. 4 shows the sensing schematic of the sensors to NH3. The gas sensing mechanism of GQDs is generally attributed to the adsorption/desorption of sensing species, which act as donors or acceptors on the GQDs surface and edge, leading to the change in conductivity of GQDs. NH3 molecules are absorbed to the hollow site through the physical adsorption mechanism without altering the electronic properties of GQDs.

Fig. 4(a) illustrates the sensing mechanism of acidic sensor A as adsorption of NH3 with the surface ionic conductivity changes. During oxidation process, COOH functional groups are attached at the edge of the GQDs as confirmed by the FTIR results shown in Fig. 1(h), leading to protonation of carboxyl groups in acid condition [15]. Thus, the initial resistance of sensor A is higher than sensor B, as shown in Fig. 3(c) and (d). When sensor A is exposed to NH3 gas with a certain humidity, the functional COOH tends to ionize to COO− and H+, then H+ ion reacts with NH3 gas to form NH4 +. As high numbers of carboxyl groups is unprotonated, the resistance of the sensor A decreases. It is worth to note that the adsorption factor of external NH3 molecules is limited by the presence of COOH functional groups at the edge of GQDs. As a result, sensor A reached its maximum response level and saturated when the concentration of ammonia is 400ppm. Since the response of sensor A is related to the physisorbed water on the sensor surface, obvious response change is observed at low relative humidity (<35%). However, the sensor A has little change of response to 10ppm ammonia under relative humidity from 35% to 80% since the response is dominated by the adsorption of NH3 molecules. Fig. 4(b) illustrates the sensing mechanism of neutral sensor B as depletion of hole carriers of GQDs due to electrons transfer from NH3 molecules to the GQDs. Surface ionic conductivity change is not considered as the main operating mechanism since the NH3 response of sensor B is independent of the humidity due to the hydrophobic sensor surface, as shown in Fig. 3(h). Generally, the neutral GQDs are considered as p-type semiconductors with holes as major charge carriers [22]. As ammonia adsorption occurred, electron-donating NH3 molecules on GQDs could cause electrons transfer from NH3 molecule to the GQDs, leading to reduced hole density in the GQDs, thereby reducing the conductance of GQDs. This deduction is in agreement to the response-NH3 concentration plots, as shown in Fig. 3(g).

In conclusion, we demonstrated a simple solution fabrication route to realize two different ammonia sensors using acidic and neutral GQDs. The GQDs were synthesized using “top-down” approaches. The pH value of aqueous GQDs was further adjusted to obtain acidic and neutral ammonia gas sensors. Both sensors show promising response and selectivity when exposed to ammonia ambient. The response of acidic GQDs sensor to ammonia gas might be attributed to the unprotonated process of carboxyl groups, while the sensing activity of neutral GQDs sensor might be attributed to the electrons transferred from NH3 molecule to GQDs, resulting in the change of hole density in the GQDs. Therefore, GQDs attached with functional groups can be used to realize ammonia gas sensors with controllable gas responses.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2013J01233), National Natural Science Foundation of China (61377027), National Research Foundation of Korea (2013-016467), State Key Laboratory of Electronic Thin Films and Integrated Devices (Grant KFJJ201309) and Foundation of Fuzhou University (2011-XY-24).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wei Chen received his Master of Engineering degree in Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics in 2007 from Fuzhou University. He is presently a Ph.D. student in Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics at Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China. His research interest comprises the development of new nanomaterials for application in electrochemical sensors and optoelectronic devices.

Fushan Li is presently a full professor in College of Physics and Information Engineering at Fuzhou University. He received his Ph.D. in Department of Physics at Peking University in 2005. His research interest focuses on the development of novel nanomaterials and their applications in optoelectronic devices.

Poh Choon Ooi received his Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering in 2013 from the Auckland University, New Zealand. He is presently a Post doctorate at Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China. His research interest comprises the theoretical and applied research in the field of polymer electronics, fabrications of nanoparticles and nanostructures.

Yun Ye is presently a professor at Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China. She received her Ph.D. at the University of Electronic Science and Technology of China in 2007. Her research comprises nano-electronic materials and devices.

Tae Whan Kim is a professor in Department of Electronic Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, Korea. He received his Ph.D. in Department of Physics from State University of New York at Buffalo in 1989. His research comprises nano-electronic materials and devices.

Tailiang Guo is a professor of Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China. He received his Master of Engineering degree at Fuzhou University in 1989. His research comprises nano-electronic materials and devices.
